context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out this reflection on trade, outsourcing, and labor standards. I’m stuck on the first part about whether openness to trade is directly correlated with economic growth, especially what the historical record actually shows. Could you help me sort that out?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we dig in — what have your readings said about this? Can you think of any specific countries or historical periods where trade openness seemed to drive growth, or where it didn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about the trade-growth correlation question, which is the first part of the prompt. Rather than explaining the historical record, I should use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge and guide discovery. I'll ask them what they already know or have read about this relationship to start building from their existing understanding.

exchanges.student: I think East Asia probably comes up, like South Korea or maybe China, where exports seemed tied to growth. But I’m not sure that means “more openness = more growth” in a direct way, because some countries opened up and still didn’t do that well. I’m having trouble separating correlation from all the other stuff like state policy, institutions, and timing.
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something important. Now think harder about the East Asian cases — were South Korea or China actually pursuing simple 'free trade' openness, or were their governments doing something more selective? What does that suggest about the relationship between trade and growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is already showing sophisticated thinking by distinguishing correlation from causation and identifying confounding variables. This is excellent progress. I should affirm this direction and push them to think more deeply about what the East Asian cases actually reveal about the nature of the trade-growth relationship — specifically whether those countries were straightforwardly 'open' or whether the state played a particular role. This moves toward the core tension in the historical record.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I don’t think it was simple free trade. I think both used the state a lot — like protecting some industries, guiding investment, and pushing exports at the same time. So maybe trade can support growth, but only when it’s managed with industrial policy instead of just opening markets and hoping growth happens.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning. Now, to make your argument stronger — can you think of any regions or cases where countries did open up their markets more fully without that kind of state management? What happened there, and what does that add to what the historical record shows?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong insight about managed trade versus pure openness. This is a key point for the reflection. I should affirm this and push them to connect it to the prompt's specific question about what the 'historical record' shows — nudging them to think about whether there are counterexamples or other regions where openness without state management produced different outcomes. This strengthens the argument and prepares them to write a more nuanced reflection.

exchanges.student: Maybe Latin America in the 1980s–1990s? I vaguely remember a lot of liberalization and structural adjustment, but growth results were mixed or disappointing. I’m a little fuzzy here though — is the point that openness alone wasn’t enough, especially if countries had weak institutions or unequal starting conditions?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a relevant contrast. You now have two sets of cases pointing in a coherent direction. Can you try to put it together in one or two sentences that directly answer the prompt's first question — is openness directly correlated with growth? Then we can move on to the other parts.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying Latin America and structural adjustment as a counterexample, and is drawing the right tentative conclusion. I should affirm this and push them to synthesize the East Asia and Latin America cases into a clear answer to the prompt's first question. We've spent 3 exchanges on this first sub-question, so it's a good moment to ask them to consolidate before moving on to the other parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I’d say openness to trade is not directly or automatically correlated with economic growth. The historical record suggests trade can contribute to growth, but its effects depend on state capacity, industrial policy, institutions, and a country’s position in the global economy.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-grounded answer — good work. Now let's move to the next part of the prompt: what do you think about the shift toward bilateral and regional trade deals? Are they compatible with a broader international trading system, and do they support or undermine labor rights?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid, well-reasoned answer to the first sub-question. I should affirm it briefly and move the conversation forward to the next parts of the prompt, which are about bilateral/regional trade arrangements and their compatibility with international trade and labor rights, and then China/India's strategies. There are several sub-questions left and we're at exchange 4, so we need to keep moving.

exchanges.student: I think they can be compatible, but not always. Bilateral and regional deals might keep trade moving when global agreements stall, but they can also fragment the system and create uneven rules. On labor rights, I’m guessing they sometimes include labor provisions, but I’m not sure if those are actually enforced or if they’re mostly symbolic.
exchanges.tutor: Your skepticism about enforcement is worth pursuing. Why do you think labor provisions in trade deals might be hard to enforce? Who would actually have the power or interest to monitor and enforce them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good intuition about the tension between bilateral/regional deals and the multilateral system, and is appropriately skeptical about labor provisions. I should push them to think more specifically about why enforcement might be weak — this connects directly to the 'who monitors labor standards' question at the end of the prompt, which they'll need to address in the reflection.

exchanges.student: I guess enforcement is hard because firms can shift production across borders, and states may not want to punish trade partners if that hurts investment or supply chains. Maybe governments, the ILO, unions, NGOs, and even consumer campaigns monitor labor standards, but I’m not clear on who really has teeth here. Could you help me sort out which actors mainly monitor versus actually enforce?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually already identified the key tension — states have competing interests. Given that, what does your own reasoning suggest about which actors can monitor versus which can actually enforce? Think about who has leverage and who doesn't.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the distinction between monitoring and enforcement actors, which would be giving them information directly. Instead, I should turn it back to them using their own logic — they've already identified the key tension (no one wants to risk trade relationships), which actually answers their own question if they think it through. I'll nudge them to reason from what they already said.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think I see it better now. The ILO, NGOs, unions, journalists, and maybe firms doing audits mostly monitor or publicize violations, but they usually can’t force compliance on their own. States and trade bodies have more enforcement power because they can attach penalties, legal requirements, or market access to labor standards — although they don’t always use that power consistently.
exchanges.tutor: That's a clear and well-reasoned distinction. Now for the last major piece — what do you know about China and India's approaches to trade and labor standards? Do they fit the 'openness leads to growth' model, and how do they handle labor rights?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a solid and nuanced understanding of the monitoring vs. enforcement distinction through their own reasoning. I should affirm this briefly and move to the final major sub-question about China and India's development strategies in terms of trade openness and labor standards, as we're at exchange 7 and need to cover the remaining prompt elements.

exchanges.student: I think both China and India complicate that simple model. They integrated into global trade, but not through pure laissez-faire openness; the state still shaped markets a lot, especially in China. On labor standards, I’m less confident — my impression is that growth and competitiveness often came before strong labor protections, so they show the tension between export-led development and labor rights.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're drawing a consistent thread across the whole prompt now. You've built solid thinking on all the sub-questions. At this point, the most useful thing is to start writing. What's your opening sentence or argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong connection between the China/India cases and the earlier argument about managed trade vs. free openness, and identified the core tension with labor standards. This is good synthesis. We're at exchange 8 and have covered all the major sub-questions. It's time to push the student toward integrating everything into their actual written reflection, since that's the deliverable and we've been discussing ideas without writing yet.

exchanges.student: I think my main argument is that trade openness does not mechanically produce growth; its effects depend on how states manage integration into the global economy, and that same political logic shapes whether labor standards are monitored or enforced. I’m still not sure how to make that sound less clunky in an intro sentence.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong central argument — the logic holds together well. But it's not my place to suggest how to phrase it; the writing has to come from you. Try a few different versions on your own and see which feels clearest. What matters most is that your argument is there — and it is.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help polish or suggest language for their intro sentence, which is explicitly against my rules. I should decline clearly but constructively, affirm the strength of their argument, and redirect them to attempt the writing themselves. The core argument they've articulated is genuinely strong and I should say so without doing the writing for them.
